title.value: OTC: Optimal Tool Calls via Reinforcement Learning
title.evidence: OTC: Optimal Tool Calls via Reinforcement Learning

authors.evidence: Hongru Wangα, Cheng Qianβ, Wanjun Zhongδ, Xiusi Chenβ, Jiahao Qiuσ, Shijue Huangµ, Bowen Jinβ, Mengdi Wangσ, Kam-Fai Wongα, Heng Jiβ

value: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
Hong Kong University of Science and Technology
affiliations.evidence: αThe Chinese University of Hong Kong, βUniversity of Illinois Urbana-Champaign σPrinceton University, δSun Yat-sen University, µHong Kong University of Science and Technology

current_issues.value: Existing methods in reinforcement learning for tool-integrated reasoning often overlook the efficiency and cost of tool usage, leading to suboptimal behavior such as excessive or insufficient tool calls. This results in increased computational and financial overhead or compromised answer quality.
current_issues.evidence: While reinforcement learning (RL) has shown promise in improving TIR by optimizing final answer correctness, existing approaches often overlook the efficiency and cost associated with tool usage. This can lead to suboptimal behavior, including excessive tool calls that increase computational and financial overhead, or insufficient tool use that compromises answer quality.

target.value: The research aims to develop a reinforcement learning framework that optimizes tool-use efficiency in tool-integrated reasoning by balancing correctness with efficient and contextually appropriate tool usage. The objective is to reduce tool calls while maintaining answer accuracy.
target.evidence: We propose Optimal Tool Call-controlled Policy Optimization (OTC-PO), a simple yet effective RL-based framework that encourages models to produce accurate answers with minimal tool calls.

framework_innovation.value: The introduction of Optimal Tool Call-controlled Policy Optimization (OTC-PO), a reinforcement learning framework that explicitly optimizes tool-use efficiency in tool-integrated reasoning. This framework is scalable, broadly applicable, and requires minimal changes to existing RL pipelines.
framework_innovation.evidence: We propose Optimal Tool Call-controlled Policy Optimization (OTC-PO), a simple yet effective RL-based method that enables large language models (LLMs) to learn precise and adaptive control over external tool usage.

metric_innovation.value: Introduction of tool productivity as a new metric to measure the effectiveness and efficiency of tool calls during inference, defined as the number of correctly answered questions per unit of tool call.
metric_innovation.evidence: Besides the exact match (EM) and the average tool calls (TC), we additionally define a new metric, tool productivity (TP), which measures the effectiveness and efficiency of tool calls during inference.

algorithm_innovation.value: Development of OTC-PPO and OTC-GRPO, which integrate the OTC-PO framework with Proximal Policy Optimization and Group Relative Policy Optimization, to enhance tool-use efficiency in reinforcement learning.
algorithm_innovation.evidence: We instantiate this framework within both Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) and Group Relative Preference Optimization (GRPO), resulting in OTC-PPO and OTC-GRPO.

performance_improvement.value: OTC-PO significantly reduces tool calls by up to 73.1% and improves tool productivity by up to 229.4% while maintaining comparable answer accuracy across multiple QA benchmarks.
performance_improvement.evidence: Experiments with Qwen-2.5 and Qwen-Math across multiple QA benchmarks show that our approach reduces tool calls by up to 73.1% and improves tool productivity by up to 229.4%, while maintaining comparable answer accuracy.

tool_call_reduction.value: OTC-PO achieves significant reductions in tool call costs, with reductions up to 73.1%, while maintaining answer accuracy, demonstrating the effectiveness of the framework in optimizing tool-use efficiency.
tool_call_reduction.evidence: Our approach reduces tool calls by up to 73.1% and improves tool productivity by up to 229.4%, while maintaining comparable answer accuracy.

model_size_effect.value: As model size increases, there is a tendency for models to over-rely on external tool calls unless explicitly penalized. Larger models benefit more from the tool-use efficiency improvements of OTC-PO.
model_size_effect.evidence: Different models have different tool use behaviors on different datasets. If we look at the Search-R1, we can find that 3B model tends to use less tool calls compared with 7B model. This reveals a critical issue: as language models scale up, they tend to over-rely on external tool calls unless explicitly penalized for such behavior.

generalization_performance.value: OTC-PO demonstrates strong generalization capabilities, outperforming baseline methods in out-of-domain evaluations with higher tool productivity and reduced tool calls.
generalization_performance.evidence: We run out-of-domain evaluation on more QA benchmarks. Figure 4 shows the results of TP and Table 4 shows the results of EM and TC. Generally, we find that our method achieves highest TP across all the model and benchmark.

case_study_insights.value: Case studies reveal that OTC-PO can reduce unnecessary tool calls while maximizing internal reasoning capabilities, leading to more efficient and accurate tool-integrated reasoning.
case_study_insights.evidence: both OTC-PPO and OTC-GRPO can reduce the number of unnecessary tool calls significantly but they achieve this in different ways. OTC-PPO utilizes more accurate search by generating information-rich query and OTC-GRPO choose to leverage the internal reasoning capabilities as much as possible since it is encouraged to solve the problem without external tool calls.

task_definition.value: The task involves generating a correct answer with minimal tool calls, given a question and an environment providing access to a set of tools. The goal is to minimize the cost of tools in the reasoning trajectory while ensuring answer correctness.
task_definition.evidence: The objective of the task is to generate the correct answer with the minimal cost of tools in the full trajectory as follows: Here the cost is measured as the number of tool calls within the trajectory considering its simplicity and generalization. Thus the model is encouraged to not only generate correct answer but also minimize the tool calls.

base_reward.value: The base reward focuses on answer correctness, assigning a reward of 1 if the predicted answer matches the ground truth, and 0 otherwise.
base_reward.evidence: r_phi(q, y) = r_correct = 1 if a = hat{a} else 0, where a is the extracted predicted answer from the response y and hat{a} is the ground truth answer.

efficiency_reward.value: The efficiency reward is designed to penalize excessive tool calls, using a cosine function to decrease the reward as the number of tool calls increases, thus encouraging fewer tool usages.
efficiency_reward.evidence: r_tool = cos(m * pi / 2m + c), where c is the smooth constant that controls the reward decay rate. The smaller the value, the faster the punishment and the more it encourages less use of tools.

final_reward.value: The final reward combines the base reward with the efficiency reward, using the tool-integrated reward function r_phi^tool(q, y) = alpha * r_tool * r_phi(q, y), ensuring that tool efficiency is rewarded when answer correctness is achieved.
final_reward.evidence: r_phi^tool(q, y) = alpha * r_tool * r_phi(q, y), where alpha is a hyperparameter that controls the scaling of the tool reward.

algorithm_integration.value: OTC-PO is integrated with Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), facilitating efficient tool use by dynamically adjusting reward signals based on tool calls and answer correctness.
algorithm_integration.evidence: We instantiate OTC-PO in two variants: OTC-PPO and OTC-GRPO, tailored to different underlying RL algorithms.

provenance.section_header: OTC-PO: Optimal Tool Call-controlled Policy Optimization

datasets.value: The experiments use NQ and HotpotQA as training datasets for search, and the dataset provided in ToRL for code.
datasets.evidence: Specifically, we use NQ and HotpotQA as training dataset for search, and we use the dataset provided in ToRL for code.

models.value: The models used in the experiments include Qwen-2.5-3B/7B-Base and Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B/7B-Base.
models.evidence: Using multiple LLMs—including Qwen-2.5-3B/7B-Base and Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B/7B-Base...

parameters.value: The parameters are reused from Search-R1 and ToRL with minor modifications. The max turn for tool calls in Search-R1 is set to 4 and in ToRL to 3.
parameters.evidence: We re-use the same parameter in Search-R1 and ToRL respectively. There are only minor modification we make to suit our method: i) We slightly change the template in Search-R1 to tell the model that it only need to call tolls when necessary (Appendix), and we do not change the template in ToRL; ii) We set the max turn (maximal number of tool calls) in Search-R1 (ToRL) to 4 (3)...

resources.value: The experiments are conducted on 8 A100-80G GPUs.
resources.evidence: We conduct our experiments on 8 A100-80G GPU...

search_tool_results.value: OTC-PO achieves significant improvements in terms of tool call reduction and tool productivity compared to Search-R1, with up to a 73.1% reduction in tool calls and a 229.4% increase in tool productivity.
search_tool_results.evidence: OTC-PPO achieves significant improvement in terms of TC and TP compared with Search-R1, and OTC-GRPO can further boost the performance due to more accurate approximation, resulting in up to a 73.1% reduction in TC and a 229.4% increase in TP...

code_tool_results.value: Preliminary results for code show similar findings to those observed in search tasks, indicating potential for tool-use efficiency improvements.
code_tool_results.evidence: Code as Tool. Table 2 shows the results for code. We already observe similar findings as mentioned above and will release more results soon.

behavior_analysis.value: OTC-PO enables faster and more efficient training optimization with fewer tool calls and shorter responses, reducing time and cost associated with real-time tool interactions.
behavior_analysis.evidence: Our method not only achieves comparable results with fewer tool calls and shorter responses, but also enables faster and more efficient training optimization.
